# Questions in Livestream 111

Live Stream #111: Questions in the Garden

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:44</a> | Why do sources say Egypt had anywhere from 30 to 33 dynasties? | He said the differences come from how later periods and foreign rulers are counted beyond Manetho's original scheme. | Falk explains that Manetho's numbered dynasties were composed before Egypt's later foreign-controlled periods were fully folded into modern schemes. Counts vary depending on whether the two Persian periods and the Ptolemies are treated as dynasties after the thirty native or traditionally numbered dynasties, so different sources can reasonably land at 30, 31, or 33. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:51</a> | If the Exodus was in 1265, how can the 430-year or 215-year chronology fit Jacob meeting a Hyksos king and Abraham in the time of Hammurabi? | He said the 430 years likely includes time in Egypt and Canaan, with the effective sojourn closer to roughly 300-350 years, which can still fit Jacob in the Hyksos period and Abraham near Hammurabi. | He argues that Exodus 12:40 is best read, on textual grounds, as covering time in both Egypt and Canaan rather than Egypt alone. Using the clan/genealogical data for Moses's family, he thinks a sojourn around 300 to 350 years is a better fit than either 215 or 430 years, allowing Jacob to fall in the Hyksos period and the patriarchal generations to reach back toward Hammurabi's era. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:47</a> | Were ancient Egyptians unable to distinguish `r` and `l`? | Largely yes. He said the sounds were weakly distinguished for much of Egyptian, with clearer separation only later. | He says Egyptian did not have a strong early distinction between the sounds represented by `r` and `l`; the mouth sign could cover both, and even the later lion sign for `l` could sometimes be read with an `r` value. He contrasts this with Hebrew, where resh and lamed are clearly distinct, and notes that the weak Egyptian distinction can complicate transliterations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:15</a> | Why is the Sphinx missing its nose? | He said nobody knows exactly why the nose disappeared, but it was already gone by the Islamic period and may have been lost through erosion, abrasion, or deliberate damage. | Falk says the Sphinx certainly once had a nose, as well as other decorative features such as a beard and painted surfaces, but the nose was already absent in Islamic-period records. Since projecting parts of statues are especially vulnerable, he lists abrasion, erosion, and intentional damage as possible causes while stressing that the exact event is unknown. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=1213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:13</a> | Was the Sphinx eroded by wind or water? | He said wind erosion explains it. | He attributes the erosion to wind-driven sand rather than water, describing the Giza Plateau as subject to severe wind abrasion. He compares the effect to a natural sandblaster that eats into soft limestone and notes that other Giza structures show deep wind-eroded damage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=1455" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:15</a> | What does he think about the authorship and dating of Deuteronomy? | He favored an early Mosaic core later arranged and linguistically updated by editors under a supplementary model. | Falk describes a supplementary model in which Moses wrote substantial core material and later priests or editors arranged, smoothed, and updated the language for readers. He places the editorial process no later than Iron Age II before the Babylonian exile, accepts obvious non-Mosaic framing such as Moses's death, and says the older Hebrew profile points earlier than prophets such as Isaiah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=1701" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:21</a> | Could biblical verses have been put in the wrong order? | He said isolated verse swapping is unlikely, but whole documents could be arranged thematically rather than chronologically. | He says the editors were handling documents, pages, and papyri rather than loose modern-style verses, so random verse-level disorder is unlikely. However, whole documents could have been placed in an order that served teaching, topic, or theme rather than strict chronology, so chapter order does not always guarantee event order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=1820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:20</a> | If a clan is four generations, was Jacob answering for his clan in Genesis 47:2, and are there ancient examples? | He said Jacob was answering for his clan, with modern Bedouin studies giving the clearest parallels for that kind of clan representation. | Falk says Jacob's answer before Pharaoh should be understood as clan representation rather than merely a modern individual age statement. He does not point to preserved nomadic ancient Near Eastern literature for this, but says ethnographic Bedouin parallels show leaders answering in terms of the life or age of the clan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=1996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:16</a> | What did he think of Zach Miller's recent interview with Matthew Lynch? | He liked the nuance and genre discussion, but thought Lynch's Egyptian-history component was weak and inconsistent with the evidence. | He praises the interview for treating conquest texts, violence, genre, and ancient war hyperbole in a nuanced way. His major objection is the Egyptian-history component: he rejects claims such as Merneptah invading in the eleventh century and thinks the idea that Israel was primarily fighting Egyptian-controlled cities is too thin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:50</a> | What exactly are eunuchs in the Bible? | He said biblical eunuchs were made through crushing trauma rather than a simple incision, and the practice was brutal and medically damaging. | Falk rejects attempts to equate biblical eunuchs with modern gender categories and describes the ancient process as physical destruction of testicular function by blunt trauma. He emphasizes the brutality of the practice, the likelihood that some did not survive it, and its use in royal or court contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:09</a> | Why not just cut when making eunuchs? | He said an incision created a much higher risk of infection, so ancient practice often used crushing instead. | He says cutting would create an open wound with a high risk of infection in ancient conditions, making death more likely. Crushing was still horrific and medically damaging, but it avoided the same incision-related infection risk and was therefore used in those ancient court practices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2582" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:02</a> | Why would making eunuchs deteriorate the bones? | He said removal or destruction of testicular function before puberty impaired testosterone-driven bone strength, producing weak bones and early osteoporosis. | Falk explains that the procedure was often done to boys around puberty, when testosterone is important for bone density during the growth spurt. Without normal testosterone, bones could grow without comparable strength, producing weak joints, early osteoporosis, and shortened lifespan; he compares this to medical observations about the castrati. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2797" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:37</a> | Was it the same for Egyptian eunuchs? | He said Egyptian eunuchs appear to have been made similarly, but were much rarer than in Mesopotamia and mainly associated with harem guards. | He says the Egyptian process was essentially similar, but Egypt used eunuchs far less widely than Mesopotamian courts. In his summary, Babylonian and Mesopotamian officials were often eunuchs, while in Egypt the practice was mainly limited to harem guards rather than general palace administration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:27</a> | Was it the same for Chinese eunuchs? | He said he did not know enough about Chinese eunuchs to answer beyond knowing that China had many of them. | Falk explicitly declines to answer the technical comparison because he does not know enough about Chinese eunuchs or their procedure. The only point he is willing to affirm is that China had many eunuchs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=2985" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:45</a> | Can he explain the difference between `nephesh`, `ruach`, and Rephaim in Hebrew? | He described `nephesh` as the living being or soul, `ruach` as spirit, wind, or life force, and Rephaim as either a people group or shades/spirits of the dead, while stressing uncertainty. | He treats `nephesh` as the living being or soul, `ruach` as spirit, wind, or life force, and Rephaim as a term with multiple possible referents: a people group, giants, or shades of the dead. He stresses that the boundaries are uncertain and suggests that in some contexts Rephaim may refer to dead or matured spirits rather than a sharply separate category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=3289" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:49</a> | What are his thoughts on the trichotomy versus dichotomy debate? | He said he has no firm view: the two-part view is simpler, but the three-part view has a compelling argument from spirit-and-soul interaction. | Falk says he has no fixed position between a body-soul model and a body-soul-spirit model. The dichotomy view appeals to him because it is simpler, but he finds the trichotomy argument interesting if soul and spirit interaction could help explain intelligence or self-talk; he concludes that Scripture is not dogmatic enough to settle the debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=3593" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:53</a> | Is spirit more like intelligence and soul more like life force in the three-part view? | He said nobody knows clearly; some assign emotions or life force to spirit and thought or memory to soul, but the terms may overlap. | He says interpreters sometimes distinguish spirit as emotion or life force and soul as thought or memory, but he does not think the Bible defines the terms that precisely. He allows that soul-spirit interaction could be a useful model for human intelligence, but keeps the claim tentative because the categories may overlap. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=3773" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:53</a> | Does 1 Samuel 28:11-14 suggest souls have the same physical appearance as our bodies and clothes? | He said spirits do not have physical bodies or clothes, but they can leave impressions that living observers mentally fill in. | Falk reads the medium's description of Samuel as an example of a spirit being recognized through impression rather than literal clothing or bodily form. He says apparition reports often involve the observer's mind filling in expected details from context, so Samuel can appear old and robed without implying that spirits have physical clothes or bodies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=4423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:43</a> | How were the giant stone boxes of the Serapeum made and moved? | He said they were carved outside and hauled into place before surrounding construction was finished, much like other large sarcophagi. | He says the Serapeum sarcophagi were made using the same general methods as other large Egyptian sarcophagi: quarry and rough-carve the stone, transport it by boat, then use sledges and levers to move it into the tomb while construction was still underway. He adds that the stones were huge but not beyond Egyptian moving capability, especially compared with obelisks or large pyramid blocks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=4728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:48</a> | Were Amram and Jochebed literally nephew and aunt in Exodus 6:20, or is this a Near Eastern idiom? | He said Hebrew kinship terms can compress generations, so the text shows relation through Amram's father's line but does not prove a literal nephew-aunt marriage. | Falk explains that Hebrew kinship language lacks the vertical precision of English terms such as grandparent or grandchild, so fathers and mothers can include ancestors across generations. Because the genealogy leaves roughly a century between Kohath and Amram, Jochebed may be related through an ancestral father's line without being Amram's literal immediate aunt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=5261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:41</a> | Is Adam understood as the literal first man or the first man God interacted with apart from other humans? | He said Adam's humanity is tied to moral responsibility and the image of God, so the biology-only framing asks the wrong question. | Falk says he does not have a firm opinion on the biological framing, because Adam's key role is as the first morally responsible human in covenantal relationship with God. He argues that humanity in Genesis is defined by the image of God, moral choice, and spiritual responsibility, so debates over possible biological ancestry can miss the larger theological question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=6494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:14</a> | Which countries are less strict with archaeology laws and allow people to take surface finds? | He mentioned Sudan and Israel as more permissive with low-value surface pottery, while stressing that taking significant artifacts is still wrong. | Falk names Sudan and Israel as examples where surface pottery or low-value finds may be treated more permissively, especially when they lack archaeological context or major value. He still frames removal of significant artifacts as morally wrong and cautions against treating permissive laws as permission to take important material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=6651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:51</a> | In Daniel 10:6, what is beryl? | He said the Hebrew `tarshish` probably refers to a precious stone like yellow or imperial topaz, though the identification remains uncertain. | He checks the Hebrew term `tarshish` and says the ancient stone identification is uncertain, but he thinks topaz is the likeliest candidate. He clarifies that ancient topaz would more likely mean a golden or imperial yellow stone, not the common modern irradiated blue topaz seen in jewelry stores. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=7131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:51</a> | How does he interpret 2 Kings 3:27, and what does he think about the Mesha Stele compared with the biblical narrative? | He said the Mesha Stele gives Moab's side of the same conflict and fits the final rally in 2 Kings 3:27, after Israel pushed beyond the coalition victory. | Falk reads the passage as a coalition campaign by Israel, Judah, and Edom against rebellious Moab, with the promised coalition victory occurring first. He thinks Mesha's sacrifice and rally correspond to the final stage in 2 Kings 3:27, where Israel goes beyond the coalition success and is forced back; the Mesha Stele gives Moab's perspective on that ending. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=7578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:18</a> | How does he connect Ezekiel 28's king of Tyre passage with the serpent in the garden? | He said the lament shifts into Eden imagery about a covering cherub, using the serpent as a type for the king of Tyre rather than identifying them as the same person. | Falk says the first part of the oracle addresses the human king, but the lament moves into language that cannot literally fit him: Eden, precious covering stones, and an anointed covering cherub. He connects that imagery to the serpent being cast down, while emphasizing that the king of Tyre is a type or analogy of the garden serpent rather than the same being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=8059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:19</a> | How prevalent is theological liberalism in secular biblical studies departments? | He said it is very common and often dominant, though that does not automatically mean the education is low quality. | He says secular biblical studies departments are very often theologically liberal, including many faculty who are only nominally Christian or who come from other religious or nonreligious commitments. He cautions that this does not automatically make the education poor, but students should know the perspective they are entering and that some departments may actively challenge faith commitments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=8280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:00</a> | Does he have a favorite monster in Egyptian myth or folklore? | He named Ammit, the soul-eater with crocodile, lion, and hippo features. | Falk names Ammit, describing her as a female demon or monster with a crocodile head, lion body, and hippopotamus hindquarters. In the weighing of the heart, failed hearts could be thrown to Ammit, making her a vivid and visually memorable Egyptian underworld figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=8418" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:18</a> | Is there any evidence that Ammit was worshiped as a god? | He said no; Ammit appears to function as an explanatory figure for the fate of impure souls, not as a worshiped deity. | He says there is no evidence of worship of Ammit: no hymns, no amulets, and no cultic material of the kind one would expect for a deity. He presents her instead as a narrative or explanatory figure for what happens to souls that fail judgment or purification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=8580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:00</a> | Is prophecy only a message from God, and what does he think of telling a neighbor to prophesy in church? | He said biblical prophecy is mostly forth-telling God's perspective, but modern claims require serious caution because false prophecy is grave. | Falk distinguishes foretelling from forth-telling, saying most biblical prophecy explains what is happening from God's perspective rather than predicting the future. He allows that the Holy Spirit can give prophecy, but warns that modern claims are often false or careless, so churches should approach prophecy with reverence, fear of the Lord, and serious concern about speaking falsely in God's name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=9294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:54</a> | What are his thoughts on Total War: Pharaoh's depiction of Amenmesse? | He said the depiction looked nothing like an Egyptian king, criticizing the headdress, kilt, collar, and other visual details. | Falk calls the depiction very poor, saying the plume, ram-horn headdress, leopard-skin kilt, belt buckle, collar, and apparent armor do not look Egyptian. He notes that Egyptians did wear kilts, but typically linen ones, and says the design does not resemble an Egyptian royal presentation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=9540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:00</a> | Could Jericho and Ai have been Egyptian outposts or client cities, as Matthew Lynch suggests? | He said the evidence is thin, with no clear support that Jericho or Ai were Egyptian outposts in the relevant period. | Falk says Egypt did have garrison cities in Canaan, but he sees no evidence that Jericho or Ai were Egyptian outposts or client cities in the relevant period. He thinks the argument requires mashing together historical data and remains the weakest part of Lynch's proposal, even though he likes other parts of the interview. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=9984" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:24</a> | Are there legitimate books from paranormal researchers he would recommend? | He mentioned *Ghosts of the Living*, Anthony Lane's *The Unseen World*, and Clinton Arnold's *Powers of Darkness*, while favoring works with careful methodology and measured skepticism. | He says some paranormal work is poor, but recommends works that combine careful method with measured skepticism. He names *Ghosts of the Living*, Anthony Lane's *The Unseen World* with its essay by David Instone-Brewer, and Clinton Arnold's *Powers of Darkness*, while distinguishing spiritual questions from more doubtful cryptozoological claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=10368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:48</a> | Could Jehovah's Witnesses be right that the figure in 1 Samuel 28 is not Samuel? | He said no, because the text repeatedly identifies the figure as Samuel. | Falk argues that the passage itself repeatedly identifies the figure as Samuel: the woman sees Samuel, Samuel speaks, and Saul responds to Samuel's words. He says a reading that makes the figure an imposter, demon, or mere appearance has to override the plain repeated identification in the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=11008" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:28</a> | Has he discussed Reformed Egyptian, and what is it? | He said Reformed Egyptian is a fictional language claimed by Joseph Smith, and that the Book of Abraham material does not match real Egyptian language or syntax. | Falk says there is no real language called Reformed Egyptian and describes it as Joseph Smith's claimed lost language. He connects the Book of Abraham material to Egyptian funerary papyri, especially Book of the Dead material, and says Smith's symbolic method and resulting text do not match actual Egyptian language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=11547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:27</a> | Could the etymology of Moses's name be just what Exodus 2 says, if Pharaoh's daughter knew Amorite in Avaris? | He said it is possible she knew Amorite, but a purely Semitic name is less likely; he prefers an Egyptian etymology that also overlaps with the Hebrew wordplay. | Falk says Pharaoh's daughter could plausibly have known Amorite in Avaris, but he doubts she would have given Moses a purely Semitic name. He prefers an Egyptian name explanation that overlaps with the Hebrew wordplay about being drawn from water, while acknowledging that more Amorite evidence would be needed to test the comparison fully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=11819" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:59</a> | Why is Ezekiel called son of man if Jesus also claims that title for himself in Daniel 7 terms? | He said son of man is not exclusive to Jesus; it can be a human title and a messianic title, and Jesus uses it with both senses in view. | He explains that "son of man" can be used for ordinary humans, as with Ezekiel, and also as a messianic Daniel 7 title. In Jesus's usage it does double duty: it affirms his full humanity while also invoking Daniel's messianic figure, allowing Jesus to use the phrase more freely than the more directly charged "Son of God." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=12245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:05</a> | Is there any correlation between Greek gods and Egyptian gods? | He said there was little original correlation, but after Alexander and the Ptolemies the Greeks deliberately equated some Egyptian gods with Greek ones. | Falk says Egyptian religion had developed for millennia before Alexander, while Greek religion followed its own path, so early direct correlation is limited. After the Greek conquest, Ptolemaic rulers and Greeks began mapping deities onto one another, such as Thoth with Hermes, Imhotep with Asclepius, and Amun-Re with Zeus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=12578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:38</a> | What would an ancient Egyptian call the year they lived in, such as around 1500 BC? | He said they used regnal dating rather than an epoch calendar, naming the king's year, season, month, and day. | Falk says Egyptians around 1500 BC would not use an epoch-style year count but would date by the reigning king's regnal year. A document might name the king, the king's year, the season, the month, and the day; he gives Amenhotep I's thirteenth year as a rough example for 1500 BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=12849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:09</a> | Was trying to talk to dead people common in the Near East during Samuel's time? | He said it varied by culture: Egypt commonly normalized speaking to the dead, while Mesopotamia treated necromancy as forbidden. | Falk says the practice depended heavily on location. Egyptians commonly spoke to the dead and even asked practical questions of deceased relatives, while Mesopotamian culture treated necromancy as forbidden; broadly, he says it became more common nearer Egypt and less common nearer Mesopotamia. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/x0lq8l-Ehvs?t=13057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:37</a> | Who were the Zadokite priests during the time of Melchizedek, and why is he king of Jerusalem? | He said Melchizedek was a Jebusite king-priest in Salem, with Canaanite divine-name background later transformed in biblical monotheistic interpretation. | Falk explains Salem/Jerusalem as a Jebusite city with Canaanite divine-name background, linking Shalem, Zedek, and El Elyon to a local religious setting. He says Melchizedek was a Jebusite king-priest in that context, and that Genesis uses this background in a way later biblical monotheism can transform typologically rather than leaving it as ordinary Canaanite religion. |
